Request Queue for DSP Requests and Responses

All requests to activate an operation or trigger in a DSP are managed by the request queue, which is an in-browser mechanism that marshals requests received from the DSP instance and dispatches them to the server or the client-side instance, as appropriate.

It is the intermediary between the client side and server side of DSP instances. For more information, see DSP Component Instances.

Requests are dispatched in the order that they are added to the queue, subject to the scope definitions of the previous requests on the queue. This can affect the sequence in which requests are submitted.

The following events, objects, and instructions add requests to the queue:

  • User events, such as editing a field or clicking a button, add requests to fire the Detail, OnChange, or OnEdit triggers.
  • DspContainer widgets add requests the fire the Detach, Attach, or OnChange triggers when the value of the containedDSP is changed.
  • The server-side ProcScript statement webactivate adds a request to fire the specified operation of the DSP instance; the requested operation can have a server or a client implementation.
  • The client-side JavaScript function activate adds a request to fire the specified operation of the specified DSP instance; the requested operation can have a server or a client implementation.
  • The client-side JavaScript function create adds a request to create a new DSP Instance of the specified DSP component and with the specified name.
  • The client-side JavaScript function remove adds a requests to delete the client part of the specified DSP instance.

Depending on the request, the queue submits the following instructions and triggers to the server or the browser, as appropriate:

  • public web trigger to execute server-side triggers (implemented in ProcScript)
  • public web operation to execute server-side operations (implemented in ProcScript)
  • webtrigger to execute client-side triggers (implemented in JavaScript)
  • weboperation to execute client-side operations (implemented in JavaScript)
Related Links
  • Race Conditions and Data Blocking

    In DSPs, all communication between the client browser and the web server is asynchronous, which can lead to data conflicts resulting from competing parallel requests and responses. Uniface enables you to define data scope declarations that can prevent these conflicts.
